text: The surgeon performs ICG fluorescence imaging on the rectal stump of a 31-year-old male patient with fulminant ulcerative colitis to assess tissue viability before implanting it into the subcutaneous tissues. The bowel is attached to the fascia to prevent pelvic sepsis, ensuring no ischemia occurs in the diseased bowel left in situ.

The surgeon divides the thickened mesentery associated with Crohn's disease using interlocking number one chromic sutures. Following this, indocyanine green (ICG) is injected to assess the viability of the end-to-side stapled ileocolic anastomosis.
